Title: Silencing of SlMYB55 affects plant flowering and enhances tolerance to drought and salt stress in tomato

Plant_Genes.Notes_or_Other_Important_Info: SlMYB55 promoter contains cis-elements responsive to ABA (ABRE), low temperature (LTR), gibberellin (P-box) and light (G-box), consistent with its hormone and stress inducibility. Silencing SlMYB55 leads to elevated basal ABA levels in leaves and increased ABA sensitivity of seedlings, indicating a role in ABA homeostasis. Despite changes in flowering time and sepal size, pollen germination vigor is not affected in SlMYB55-RNAi lines.
Plant_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: SlMYB55 acts as a negative regulator of drought and salt tolerance and a regulator of flowering and floral organ development; silencing SlMYB55 enhances salt and drought tolerance, advances flowering, increases flower number, and produces smaller flowers and sepals.
Plant_Genes.Regulatory_Mechanism: SlMYB55 is an R2R3-MYB transcription factor whose expression is induced by ABA, salt and drought. It directly binds and regulates the promoters of WUS and 4CL, and physically interacts with the MADS-box protein MBP21. Silencing SlMYB55 leads to altered expression of multiple stress-related genes (Cat1, Cat2, LEA/TAS14, P5CS, GME2, ER5) and inflorescence/flowering genes (S, TMF, LS, FA, SP, AP2a, SOC1, LFY, WUS, MBP21), indicating it functions as a transcriptional regulator integrating ABA signaling, stress responses and floral development.
Plant_Genes.Regulatory_Pathway: ABA-dependent osmotic stress signaling pathway; stress-responsive antioxidant and osmoprotectant pathways; inflorescence and flower meristem development pathway; flavonoid biosynthesis pathway.
Plant_Genes.Biosynthetic_Pathway: Flavonoid biosynthesis via regulation of 4CL (4-coumaroyl CoA ligase); influences ABA biosynthesis/signaling.
Plant_Genes.Upstream_or_Downstream_Regulation: Direct/primary regulation: SlMYB55 --(direct transcriptional regulation; binding to promoter MBS motifs)--> WUS; SlMYB55 --(direct transcriptional regulation; binding to promoter MBS motifs)--> 4CL. In SlMYB55-RNAi inflorescences, WUS expression is strongly downregulated (20–40% of WT), whereas 4CL expression is significantly upregulated, indicating positive regulation of WUS and negative regulation of 4CL by SlMYB55. Protein interaction: SlMYB55 --(protein–protein interaction; yeast two-hybrid)--> MBP21 (MADS-box protein), suggesting cooperative regulation of sepal size. Indirect/associated regulation under salt stress: silencing SlMYB55 leads to higher induction (2–6.5-fold over WT) of stress-related genes Cat1, Cat2, LEA (TAS14), P5CS, GME2 and ER5. Indirect regulation of inflorescence/flowering genes: in SlMYB55-RNAi inflorescences, S, TMF and LS are downregulated, while FA, SP, AP2a, SOC1, LFY and MBP21 are upregulated, consistent with altered inflorescence architecture, flowering time and floral organ size.
Plant_Genes.Interacting_Proteins: MBP21 (MADS-box family protein), shown to physically interact with SlMYB55 by yeast two-hybrid assay.
Plant_Genes.Expression_Pattern: SlMYB55 is expressed in all tested tomato organs with highest levels in stems, moderate levels in immature and mature green fruits and flowers, and low levels in young leaves and sepals. Its transcript level is transiently induced by ACC, ABA, IAA, MeJA and GA3, peaking at 8 h (ACC, GA3), 12 h (ABA, MeJA) and 24 h (IAA). Under 300 mM NaCl and dehydration, SlMYB55 expression is induced, with NaCl causing a 1.34- to 6.35-fold increase by 8 h and drought induction evident by 12 h.
Plant_Genes.Key_Environment_or_Treatment_Factor: Salt stress (NaCl 40–100 mM on MS medium, 300 mM NaCl irrigation in soil); drought stress (withholding water up to 21 days; dehydration of detached leaves); hormone treatments including ABA (3–100 μM), ACC, MeJA, GA3 and IAA; ABA-containing media for sensitivity assays.
Plant_Genes.Research_Topic: Regulation of drought and salt stress tolerance and flower development by the MYB transcription factor SlMYB55 in tomato
Plant_Genes.Trait_Category: Abiotic stress tolerance (salt, drought); flowering time; inflorescence and flower development
Plant_Genes.Quantitative_Phenotypic_Alterations: SlMYB55 transcript levels in RNAi lines are reduced by 85–96% in stems relative to WT. Under 100 mM NaCl, RNAi seedlings have hypocotyls and roots approximately 12 mm and 8 mm longer, and seedling weight about 7 mg higher than WT after 7 days. Under 80 mM NaCl, WT seeds hardly germinate, whereas RNAi lines reach 29–33% germination by 13 days. After 15 days of 300 mM NaCl, chlorophyll content in WT leaves decreases by ~72% versus ~34–51% in RNAi lines; relative conductivity increases to ~24% and 39% in WT at 7 and 15 days versus ~11–14% and 26–27% in RNAi lines. Under drought, detached leaf water loss at 210 min is 53.86% in WT versus 32.31–40.01% in RNAi lines. With 15 and 21 days of drought, leaf RWC in WT drops to ~75% and 38%, versus ~84–87% and 59–72% in RNAi lines; MDA increases are consistently lower in RNAi lines. WUS expression in RNAi inflorescences is reduced to 20–40% of WT, while FA, SP, AP2a, MBP21, SOC1 and LFY are upregulated by ~1.26–2.86-, 2.77–4.80-, 2.51–3.46-, 1.56–6.35-, 9.46–23.26- and 2.31–5.26-fold, respectively. Under 15 days of salt stress, stress-related genes Cat1, Cat2, LEA (TAS14), P5CS, GME2 and ER5 are induced 2- to 6.5-fold higher in RNAi roots than in WT.
Plant_Genes.Other_Phenotypic_Effects: SlMYB55-RNAi lines show improved seed germination under salt stress, reduced membrane damage and lipid peroxidation under salt and drought, better water retention of detached leaves, increased endogenous ABA content and heightened sensitivity to exogenous ABA, while pollen germination remains unaffected.
Plant_Genes.Experimental_Materials: Wild-type tomato (Solanum lycopersicum cv. Ailsa Craig, AC++); independent T2 SlMYB55-RNAi lines (RNAi06, RNAi08, RNAi09); tobacco plants for transient expression; yeast strain Y2H Gold for yeast two-hybrid assays.
Plant_Genes.Experimental_Methods: Gene cloning and sequence analysis of SlMYB55; promoter cis-element prediction; quantitative real-time PCR for tissue, hormone and stress-induced expression; construction of SlMYB55-RNAi vector and Agrobacterium-mediated transformation of tomato; salt stress assays on MS medium (germination, seedling growth) and in soil (300 mM NaCl irrigation); drought assays by withholding water and detached leaf water-loss measurements; physiological measurements including chlorophyll content, relative electrical conductivity, MDA content and relative water content; ABA quantification by HPLC and ABA sensitivity assays on ABA-containing medium; in vitro pollen germination assays; tobacco transient expression assays with SlMYB55 effector and WUS/4CL promoter-GUS reporters; yeast two-hybrid assays to test SlMYB55–MBP21 protein interaction; statistical analysis with Student’s t-test.
Plant_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Stable RNA interference (SlMYB55-RNAi) in tomato followed by salt and drought tolerance assays and flowering/flower phenotyping; qRT-PCR analysis of stress-related and flower development genes; tobacco transient expression to test direct regulation of WUS and 4CL promoters; yeast two-hybrid analysis of SlMYB55–MBP21 protein interaction.
Plant_Genes.Gene_Discovery_or_Cloning_Method: Candidate MYB transcription factor identified from tomato genomic data (GenBank XM_004248925); cloned based on known sequence, followed by promoter analysis and expression profiling under hormones and stress to infer function, and reverse genetics via RNAi to validate its role.
Plant_Genes.Breeding_Application_Value: Silencing SlMYB55 improves tomato tolerance to drought and high salinity while advancing flowering and increasing flower number, making SlMYB55 a promising molecular target to develop stress-resilient tomato cultivars with modified flowering behavior and floral organ size. The gene’s direct regulation of WUS and 4CL and its integration of ABA-dependent stress responses suggest it can be manipulated to enhance stress tolerance through improved antioxidant capacity and ABA signaling without compromising pollen viability, providing a basis for tomato improvement via transgenic or genome-editing approaches.
